title: Renormalization Group and Homogenization
speaker: Scott Armstrong
abstract: I will describe some new "coarse-graining" methods in quantitative homogenization and how they can be used to give rigorous versions of certain heuristic "renormalization group" arguments in physics, with a focus on several examples.

proof_stages.label: Anomalous-dissipation problem for passive scalars
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up the advection-diffusion equation with rough incompressible drift, derives the basic energy identity, defines anomalous dissipation/spontaneous stochasticity, and frames the phenomenon as a transfer of low modes to dissipative high modes.

proof_stages.label: Theorem and contrast with earlier mechanisms
proof_stages.summary: After reviewing stochastic and deterministic precedents, Armstrong states the main theorem constructing a spacetime-continuous Hölder drift with exponent below one third for which anomalous dissipation occurs along an explicit subsequence for every initial datum.

proof_stages.label: Renormalized finite-scale homogenization strategy
proof_stages.summary: The proof strategy is to erase progressively smaller velocity wiggles by homogenization, replacing them with larger effective diffusivities, and to compare the dissipations of successive renormalized equations until order-one diffusion appears at the top scale.

proof_stages.label: Skew-potential formulation and enhanced diffusivity mechanism
proof_stages.summary: The drift term is rewritten as a divergence-form operator with a skew-symmetric matrix potential, allowing homogenization theory and elementary flow calculations to show how molecular diffusivity can be amplified by cellular or shear motions.

proof_stages.label: Scale recursions and the one-third threshold
proof_stages.summary: The construction introduces active length scales, time scales, and shear amplitudes whose recurrence relations and spatial/time homogenization constraints force super-geometric scale separation and the Hölder restriction beta less than one third.

proof_stages.label: Lagrangian multiscale vector-field construction
proof_stages.summary: A naive sum of alternating shears is shown to fail because larger-scale drifts wash out small-scale enhancement, and the remedy is to advect each new fine-scale shear by the inverse flow of the already constructed larger-scale field.

proof_stages.label: Rigorous closure, limitations, and extensions
proof_stages.summary: The closing discussion identifies the contribution as making an old renormalization-group turbulence heuristic rigorous despite finite scale separation, explains the dependence on sharp periodic homogenization estimates, and points toward Euler-compatible and random/SPDE extensions.

examples.name: Navier-Stokes turbulent energy cascade
examples.note: The Navier-Stokes picture of forcing at large scales and dissipation at small scales motivates what anomalous dissipation should mean, but it is not used in the construction.
examples.engagement_reason: the Navier–Stokes energy cascade is used as motivating turbulence picture, with no concrete calculation or construction carried out on a specific flow

examples.name: Kraichnan white-in-time passive-scalar model
examples.note: The Kraichnan model illustrates a known stochastic mechanism for anomalous diffusion and serves mainly as a contrast because its white-in-time velocity field supplies too much randomness.
examples.engagement_reason: the Kraichnan white-in-time model is historically described and facts about Ito/closed correlations are stated, but no actual correlation equation or computation is worked through

passes.reason: Kraichnan’s Gaussian model is the singular zero-correlation-time/white-in-time limiting case used as prior evidence, not as a refutation
The Kraichnan model is a zero-correlation-time singular limit used as a known model where the naive vanishing-dissipation expectation fails.
The Kraichnan model is the zero-correlation-time Dirac/white-noise limit and is invoked to show anomalous diffusion, i.e. failure of the vanishing-dissipation heuristic.
The Kraichnan model is a singular zero-correlation-time limiting velocity field and is cited as producing anomalous diffusion against the vanishing-dissipation intuition.
The Kraichnan model has a singular white-in-time, Dirac-correlated temporal structure, and is cited as prior positive evidence for anomalous diffusion.

a singular white-in-time Gaussian model, not a genuine time-regular velocity field, used as a solvable historical model rather than an adversarial example.

examples.name: Checkerboard shearing mixer from prior deterministic work
examples.note: The alternating-shear construction that turns a checkerboard into finer checkerboards illustrates the earlier perturbative-mixing mechanism that Armstrong contrasts with his simultaneous multiscale cascade.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker traces the shearing construction on the checkerboard, explaining how successive shears make smaller checkerboards and how diffusion finally blurs and kills them

passes.reason: the alternating-shear checkerboard construction is a highly regular self-similar mixer whose cascade collapses into a final-time singular mechanism

examples.name: Cellular vortex flow as a random walk between cells
examples.note: The periodically repeated cellular flow makes concrete how small diffusion can trigger jumps between stream cells and thereby yield enhanced effective diffusivity, but the proof later uses a different shear-flow construction.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker reasons through the cellular vortex particle motion, boundary-layer jumps between cells, random-walk behavior, and the resulting effective diffusivity scaling
